title: BANKS BEHIND HOME CRISIS
full_text:
The Australian housing crisis is our deepest structural problem. The banking system is at the heart of the crisis, so as the crisis deepens it is going to impact the performance of the ASX and superannuation balances.
The social problems being created are immense, but so is the involvement of people linked to the CFMEU and other building unions. The federal government did not address the causes of the crisis but merely addressed some of the symptoms, which made it worse.
On Tuesday and Wednesday I am going to document the causes of the crisis and suggest solutions that are very different from current government policy.
My commentary will be based on a 10,000-word analysis of the problem by John Dahlsen, who is a former ANZ director and chief of one of Australia’s largest building suppliers.
Few understand the links between the banking and building industries better than Dahlsen. As Jim Chalmers’ budget showed, there is no such knowledge in Treasury.
Dahlsen points out that Australia is attempting to cram approximately 11 million households into 10 million homes. It needs between 250,000 and 300,000 new dwellings a year, but in 2025 building starts came in at only 196,000.
Australia now has the highest level of mortgage stress in the developed world. Sydney households need 62.6 per cent of income to service a new mortgage. The rental vacancy rate has collapsed below 1 per cent. Dahlsen points out that measures being undertaken simultaneously inflate housing demand, constrain supply and transfer wealth from the young and the enterprising to the established and the protected.
Once the reasons this is happening are understood, the solutions become obvious. We will start with the banking system.
APRA’s capital framework treats residential mortgages as less risky than commercial and development lending, so banks drive mortgage lending hard while rationing development finance.
The result is a system that simultaneously inflates demand through abundant mortgage credit and constrains supply through tight construction lending. Australia has by far the largest proportion of residential mortgages as a share of bank assets of any comparable country.
Generous mortgage lending, set against the few homes actually being built, inflates the price of existing housing. Inflated values then justify still larger borrowings.
In a downturn – like the one we are experiencing – the banks’ mortgage books will come under pressure. Because of APRA’s conservatism on capital, banks will hold sufficient buffers to absorb the decline but our superannuation funds will wake up to the fact that their index-based investing means bank shares have not yet fully adjusted to the new circumstances.
In summary, Australia has too much household debt, with banks that have lent too heavily into mortgages and too cautiously into the construction sector that would relieve the housing shortage.
Fixing that problem requires a total change in the regulator, APRA, which, incredibly, is funded by the banks and mostly staffed by ex-bankers. Transforming APRA and the banking rules (along the lines originally recommended by the Productivity Commission) is merely the first step. Bankers and APRA will fight hard against change because it will mean more banking competition and different APRA staffing.
But simply instructing banks to lend more to builders would be a disaster because the building sector is in deep crisis. The rate of builder failure is high and developers are simply not developing because they cannot make projects viable.
A building development proceeds only when the expected revenue exceeds the full stack of costs: land, construction, holding and finance costs, and a margin large enough to compensate for the risk being taken.
When construction and finance costs rise faster than achievable sale prices, projects no longer stack up, and rational developers shelve them or require government subsidy.
Meanwhile, bankers demand fixed-price building contracts before they will lend. Builders are then forced to take the rising cost risk. Once again, builders have been caught between fixed-price contracts signed before recent cost spikes and the much higher costs of actually delivering them. This is a direct path to insolvency.
Dahlsen, as a supplier, is in the frontline of this development and says we have the makings of stagflation in the sector because builder and developer revenues are falling while their costs are rising.
The result is fewer builders, fewer developers, and fewer employees and subcontractors available to do the work.
Housing starts will not increase materially because the conditions required to unlock a significant increase in new dwelling supply (developer confidence, accessible finance, affordable construction costs and a skilled workforce) are all deteriorating simultaneously. This creates a supply problem.
The tragedy is that many of the policies being adopted do little for supply while actively inflating demand. These demand-side measures are designed to harvest votes rather than to act in the national interest and confront the underlying issues.
This has been further exacerbated by a very high rate of immigration. The failure by the state governments and the federal government to manage the intake has contributed to persistent housing availability and affordability challenges, and has also undermined community support for migration.
At recent peaks, net overseas migration has run at a level roughly equivalent to adding a city about twice the size of Geelong every year.
It is little wonder that our hospitals, schools, roads and ambulance services struggle to keep pace. Dwelling supply has fallen well short of the new demand.
Currently, the federal government does not enforce a limit on long-term arrivals to Australia. Long-term migrants compete with Australians and one another for housing, resulting in higher prices and fewer available dwelling units. I will address other issues and solutions on Wednesday.
Vale Derryn Hinch I am proud to have had a small role in the radio success of Derryn. In 1979 I was on the board of Melbourne’s 3AW that hired him from a rival radio station and kept his morale up when his initial ratings were lower than those of his predecessor. And together we worked out a way to challenge the then ban on political broadcasting 48 hours before an election without endangering the station’s licence. He broadcast, but I would press the “stop” button.
